mnemonics: Alpha-bet Soup: α (alpha) is the significance level you set in your 'soup' before you test, like a recipe for rejection.
keyPoints: The significance level (α) is the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true (a Type I error).
Commonly used levels are α = 0.05 (5%) and α = 0.01 (1%), representing the threshold for what is considered statistically significant.
A lower α value (e.g., 0.01) makes it harder to reject the null hypothesis, thereby reducing the chance of a false positive but increasing the chance of a false negative (Type II error).
commonMistakes: Confusing the significance level (α) with the p-value. α is set before the experiment; the p-value is calculated from the data.
Believing a p-value lower than α 'proves' the alternative hypothesis is true. It only indicates the data is inconsistent with the null hypothesis under that specific α threshold.
